region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent (North India); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general naan bread entry (FOODON_03315021); no specific cheese-garlic-stuffed variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers flat bread as a category. No specific naan or cheese-garlic naan code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for cheese garlic naan or naan bread identified in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of cheese garlic naan weighing approximately 120 g, as typically served in an Indian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Naan, Indian flatbread with cheese and garlic — composite estimate derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (naan base) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cheese and garlic filling components
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for cheese garlic naan as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for plain naan (maida-based leavened flatbread, ~280 kcal/100 g, protein ~8 g, fat ~4 g, carbohydrate ~55 g) with USDA SR Legacy data for processed cheese and garlic filling contribution, weighted at approximately 20% filling by mass. The resulting composite per-100 g values are consistent with published ranges for stuffed naan in peer-reviewed Indian dietary studies. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of Indian breads and flatbreads — naan (white flour, tandoor-baked) GI approximately 71; analogous estimate applied to cheese garlic naan variant
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database lists plain naan (white flour, tandoor-baked) with a GI of approximately 71 (high). Cheese garlic naan uses the same refined wheat flour base; the cheese and garlic filling contributes fat and protein which may modestly lower the effective GI, but no direct measurement exists for the stuffed variant. GI of 71 is retained as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (71 × 55.8) / 100 ≈ 39.6, rounded to 39.8 using 46.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.2 serving = 55.8 g carbohydrate per 120 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~504 mg per standard 120 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit cheese garlic naan to one piece per meal and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes such as curries or chutneys, as the combined sodium load can approach or exceed the recommended 1500–2000 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~71) and high glycemic load per serving (~39.8) due to refined wheat flour base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat cheese garlic naan as an occasional food; the high GI and glycemic load from refined maida flour can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation, and portion should be restricted to half a piece paired with high-fibre vegetables or legumes to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.2 g per 100 g) from cheese and butter/ghee, combined with moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume cheese garlic naan in moderation; the saturated fat from cheese and ghee contributes to LDL cholesterol elevation, and the sodium content adds to blood pressure burden, both of which are independent cardiovascular risk factors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese Garlic Naan is a popular North Indian leavened flatbread made from refined wheat flour (maida) that is hand-stretched, stuffed with a mixture of cheese (typically paneer or processed cheese) and minced garlic, then baked at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The result is a soft, slightly charred bread with a fluffy interior, a rich cheesy filling, and a pronounced garlic aroma. It is finished with a brush of butter or ghee, giving it a glossy, indulgent appearance. In Singapore, cheese garlic naan is widely served in Indian restaurants as an accompaniment to curries, dal, and grilled meats, and is also enjoyed on its own as a snack. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense (~295 kcal per 100 g) with a moderate protein content (~9.5 g) from both the wheat and cheese, a meaningful fat contribution (~7.8 g, of which ~4.2 g is saturated) from the cheese and butter, and a high carbohydrate load (~46.5 g) dominated by refined starch. The glycemic index is high (~71), making it a food of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) is relevant for hypertension management. Calcium (~145 mg per 100 g) from the cheese filling is a positive micronutrient contribution. Clinically, this food is best consumed in controlled portions by individuals with metabolic conditions.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for cheese garlic naan as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for plain naan with USDA SR Legacy data for cheese and garlic filling components. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain white-flour naan. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation methodology.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy source data for this composite estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this composite estimate. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this composite estimate. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the cited sources. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Dairy and eggs'; this has been corrected to 'Grains and staples' as naan is primarily a grain-based flatbread product, with dairy as a filling component. Serving size of 120 g is based on typical restaurant portion for one piece of naan as observed in Singapore Indian restaurant contexts. Glycemic load per serving = (71 × 46.5 × 1.2) / 100 = 39.8.
